skill.name: xlsx
skill.description: Comprehensive spreadsheet creation, editing, and analysis with support for formulas, formatting, data analysis, and visualization. When Claude needs to work with spreadsheets (.xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, .tsv, etc) for: (1) Creating new spreadsheets with formulas and formatting, (2) Reading or analyzing data, (3) Modify existing spreadsheets while preserving formulas, (4) Data analysis and visualization in spreadsheets, or (5) Recalculating formulas
skill.summary: Comprehensive spreadsheet creation, editing, and analysis with support for formulas, formatting, dat...
skill.author: ZhanlinCui

security_audit.summary: All 57 static findings are false positives. The skill uses subprocess to run LibreOffice for formula recalculation, which is legitimate spreadsheet automation. The filesystem operations access standard config directories. The SKILL.md contains code examples and Excel references (cell names like C2, error types like #REF!) that were incorrectly flagged.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Commands - Legitimate LibreOffice Usage
dangerous_patterns.description: subprocess.run used to run LibreOffice for formula recalculation - legitimate spreadsheet automation. Commands are hardcoded with no user input injection.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Hardcoded commands for LibreOffice automation - standard spreadsheet tool usage

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem Access - Standard Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: Access to ~/.config/libreoffice for macro configuration - standard application configuration storage, not a security concern.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Standard config directory access for LibreOffice macro setup

dangerous_patterns.title: Documentation Code Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Shell command examples in SKILL.md documentation - not executable code, just documentation showing usage patterns.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Code examples in markdown documentation, not actual shell execution

dangerous_patterns.title: Excel Cell References Misidentified
dangerous_patterns.description: Cell references like 'C2' and error types like '#REF!' in SKILL.md were misidentified as security issues - these are Excel formula syntax.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: C2 is an Excel cell reference, #REF! is an Excel error type - not security issues

content.value_statement: This skill enables Claude Code to create, edit, and analyze Excel spreadsheets with formulas, formatting, and data visualization. It solves the problem of manual spreadsheet manipulation by automating complex spreadsheet operations.
actual_capabilities: Create new Excel files with formulas, formatting, and styling
Read and analyze data from existing Excel files using pandas
Modify existing spreadsheets while preserving formulas and formatting
Recalculate formulas using LibreOffice and detect errors
Apply color coding standards for financial models
Generate formatted reports with charts and data visualization
limitations: LibreOffice must be installed for formula recalculation
Complex Excel features like macros and VBA are not supported
Very large files may have performance constraints

use_cases.title: Financial model creation
use_cases.description: Build complete financial models with assumptions, projections, and formatted output following industry standards

use_cases.title: Data reporting automation
use_cases.description: Automate data export and reporting workflows by generating formatted Excel reports from raw data

use_cases.title: Spreadsheet error correction
use_cases.description: Identify and fix formula errors like #REF!, #DIV/0!, and #VALUE! in existing spreadsheets

prompt_templates.title: Create simple spreadsheet
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a new Excel file called sales_report.xlsx with columns for Date, Product, Quantity, and Price. Add 5 sample rows of data.

prompt_templates.title: Build financial model
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a financial model for a SaaS company with assumptions sheet, revenue projections for 5 years, and formatted output. Use blue for inputs, black for formulas.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze existing data
prompt_templates.prompt: Read the data from data.xlsx, analyze the trends, and create a summary sheet with key statistics and charts.

prompt_templates.title: Fix formula errors
prompt_templates.prompt: Open workbook.xlsx, recalculate all formulas, identify any errors, and fix the #REF! and #DIV/0! errors found.

output_examples.input: Create a quarterly sales report with charts
output: Created Q1_sales_report.xlsx with 3 sheets: Summary, Details, Charts
Added conditional formatting to highlight top performers
Generated bar chart showing sales by product category
Created dcf_model.xlsx with Assumptions, Revenue, FCF, and Valuation sheets
Applied color coding: blue for inputs, black for formulas, green for cross-sheet refs
Built 5-year projection with NPV and IRR calculations
All formulas validated - model ready for sensitivity analysis

best_practices: Always use Excel formulas instead of hardcoded values to keep spreadsheets dynamic
Run recalc.py after creating or modifying files with formulas to verify no errors
Follow financial model color standards: blue for inputs, black for formulas, green for sheet links
anti_patterns: Calculating values in Python and hardcoding them instead of using Excel formulas
Saving files without running recalc.py, leaving formulas uncalculated
Using data_only=True when loading files that need formula preservation

faq.question: How do I recalculate formulas in an Excel file?
faq.answer: Use the recalc.py script: python recalc.py yourfile.xlsx. It will recalculate all formulas and report any errors.

faq.question: Can I edit existing Excel files without breaking formulas?
faq.answer: Yes, openpyxl preserves formulas when editing. Just be careful not to overwrite cells containing formulas with new values.

faq.question: What libraries does this skill use?
faq.answer: openpyxl for formula and formatting operations, pandas for data analysis, and LibreOffice via recalc.py for formula calculation.

faq.question: How do I add charts to Excel files?
faq.answer: Use openpyxl.chart module to create chart objects and attach them to worksheet cells. Refer to openpyxl documentation for chart types.

faq.question: Why are formulas showing as text instead of values?
faq.answer: openpyxl saves formulas as strings. Run python recalc.py to calculate all formula values using LibreOffice.

faq.question: Can I use this skill with CSV or TSV files?
faq.answer: Yes, pandas can read and write CSV and TSV files. For formula support, use XLSX format.
